author: @kellyvcraig
text:
A more simple approach that soldering the plate onto the connector would just be winding some 14 or 12 gauge copper wire around the end. 

You could file a slight grove about an inch back and start the wind in it, to keep the copper from sliding off. A light tap or three with a hammer might seat it a bit better.

replies.author: @mycrowpeteandus
replies.text: If you follow the method Mr. Murray-Smith is kindly detailing for his viewers, the series just prior to this detailed the all-in-one solution that is used with most any metal shoe, where that saves quite a bit with not needing the sacrificial anode to make the gold solution, platinum solution, ect. I am detailing this so that other viewers are able to make sense of why doing it Mr. Murray-Smith's way is the proper way when following his extremely inexpensive method in its entirety, specific metal shoes included.

author: @dwightgordon9354
text: If you wanted to make the wand for gold plating, how much gold would be required for the shoe?  I ask because I think I might have just found a purpose for the tiny amounts of gold you can get from e-scrapping.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very little mate - as you will be putting a very thin coat on

replies.author: @dwightgordon9354
replies.text: Thank you so much for the quick reply.  Let me push you just a little bit more for more specifics, if you would please.  I have seen several videos on e-scrapping for gold recovery, and have a fair amount saved up.  If I have the same level of success as the videos show, I should have between 0.7 and 0.9 grams of pure gold when I am done.  If I hammered that down for the shoe portion of the wand, how much do you suppose I would be able to do with it?

author: @ffaber25
text: hello mr robert I'm in a project regarding my study I bought the chemical inputs but at higher concentrations, although I have not done the mixtures, so far I have compared the solution of video with other solutions on the internet and think it works, but not is the nikel, in two weeks it'll take to experimental, I will close with high amperage 50 amp to increase the thickness of the layers, I wonder when using more concentrated components can be problems in electroplating?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - electroplating baths can be very sensitive to pH, temperature current and concentration - but that is why experimentation is important - good luck

author: @K9JT
text:
It's so difficult to find good DIY videos for simple and inexpensive brush plating methods. Even though it's a relatively old series, it's still the most valuable I've found, so thank you!
A few questions if you have the time to answer...
1. Does the metal surface still need to be activated before brushing with this solution?
2. If the solution "eats" the metal under a current, can I just dissolve the plating metal beforehand and just dip the sponge in that? Or even make an electric highlighter type thing and fill it with the dissolved metal solution? (to eliminate dipping and provide a constant flow)
3. Do you know anything about colloidal gold. It seems like a lot simpler process for dissolving gold using only table salt, sodium citrate and distilled water. (Making Colloidal Gold from Gold Wire and Electrolysis) I know it's used mainly for "medicinal" purposes, but I was thinking that since it's just suspended gold nanoparticles, could it be used for plating as well?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya mate, thanks - it is an old one yes - but, there is no need to activate as far as I know or remember - I would certainly try number 2 and I don't know much about colloidal gold but i don't particularly see why not

replies.author: @K9JT
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Great. I really want to try make the electric highlighter now. Could use just a pen with a felt tip and stick a wire inside. It could even be battery operated because you only need between 3-9v.
Does your electrolyte solution only work well with copper, brass, zinc, gold and silver, or can I use it for iron as well? I'm interested in playing around with your conductive ink & copper & iron & magnets :-D
And I'm definitely going to try colloidal gold and will let you know. Just have to "find" some gold first...

author: @allyoops7020
text: Hello Robert. Fascinating stuff.  And I think many of us are grateful for bringing this method to us.  I also downloaded the patent you referred to and will try to replicate.  I am new to this.  In the near future I will sweat together gold to a wand.  My concern is when the other metals like the lead solder and the other metals are exposed to the electroplating solution is this also transferred on to the piece being plated??? In other words how can the gold anode be exposed to the solution without exposing the solder and wire in the wand?  Ally

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Hiya Mate,

If it was a bath process you would be right to be concerned - but it is a contact process and only that metal in contact will transfer

author: @AndyDaviesByTheSea

Hi Robert like the last comment I was looking for the next video.
What I do when I make a series is to copy the link from the next video and paste it into the ‘description/show more’ of each of the videos in that series. It’s a bit of a pain as if I make a thirteen part video as each time I make the next episode I paste in the links for each video in the series it means that you can find any video from any video your watching. Anyway, just a thought.
Kind Regards ... Andy

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: is 1 amp per square centimeter. if you double the area you half the current density. Equally if you double the voltage you double the current density and so on. So, the effect of this is that you can play around with voltage, amperage and resistance to get the correct density for plating whatever you use. Normally, within reason, the resistance is fixed and you play around with amps and volts and area to get it right. If you google the materials you want to plate the correct current density is

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: 12V is fine. I hope I am not teaching my grandmother to suck eggs here but i thought it might help to say there is a relationship between current, voltage and resistance. Ohms law it is V=IR where V= volts, I= current and R= resistance. With electroplating what you are interested in is the current density. That is the amount of current supplied per square centimeter. So, if your area is 1cm squared and you supply 1 amp at 1 volt and the cirucit has a resistance of 1 ohm then the current density
